item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's real compassion in the interrogation log here, and I love to see it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here, and it's nearly bed time.

+1, now stay out of my dreams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not gonna lie…….the chair is what did it for me…..The interview as a whole, but especially the chair

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I put it on my art thread a couple days ago, lol. I hope you'll see it.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Great world building and horror. The idea of being having a manifestation of your nightmares appear as you sleep and infect others is quite good.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I look forward to seeing it. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll definitely be making art of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, what are the odds of that.

Glad the picture could be such a great tool for eliciting the sense of horror I wanted in the skip.

Thanks for the +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have that CPAP.

That exact model.

Take your +1 and let me sleep, you monster.

item.title: Re: Update 4/21/17
item.content:encoded: I'm honored to take part in this. I remember at the start of the contest, I saw Sleep Paralysis and worried I'd have too much overlap with Falling Out. But after reading it, I realized we had just the right amount of overlap.

item.title: Update 4/21/17
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't be the member of the Wikiwalk team that I am if i didn't link this article to yet more articles on the site. In this case, and with WrongJohnSilver's permission, I have linked SCP-3060 to the very excellent SCP-3966, making this the first intra-series link of Series IV, I believe.

Why this link? Well, from a narrative stand point both articles are very compatible as they deal with the shenanigans that occur when entities interested in human sleep start shaking things up. From the standpoint of the story being told in this SCP…

+ Spoiler
- Hide Spoiler

The entities responsible for SCP-3060 are both highly organized, and highly prepared. They have broken into the Oneiroi Headspace user data, and using that app have recruited several real life pawns, like Dr. Zhou, the Portland Sleep Medicine staff, and the Kervier warehouse employees. If such pawns become liabilities, its a simple matter to mark them and make them easier targets for the SCP-3966 dream spiders to off. No fuss no muss.

Why go through all that trouble? Because if your an entity that survives off parasitizing individual's headspace, SCP-3060 is a means of providing you and your fellow entities with your next meal.

item.title: Sleep Paralysis is now SCP-3060
item.content:encoded:
In keeping with my naming scheme that has been around since I first wrote my now deleted SCP-1160 and SCP-1260, I have taken the 3060 spot for my 3000 contest entry.

While its not the 3000 spot, to me this is a very good consolation prize.

Thanks for all who voted, commented, and read this during the contest.

That is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny thing: AK-class is exactly what came to mind when I was reading it, but I didn't want to try to force headcanon on anyone.

I didn't see it as headcanon forcing at all. How the world ends in an AK scenario vs XK is as radically different as a mass outbreak of SCP-571 vs the Earth running into SCP-1548, and that is totally a thing to call out, and I'm glad you did. In the end such a change does make this a slightly stronger article.

:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to level with you, it wasn't until after the editing period ended that I found the more fitting AK-class End of World Scenario, which is arguably a much better fit than straight up XK-class.

Sounds good! Funny thing: AK-class is exactly what came to mind when I was reading it, but I didn't want to try to force headcanon on anyone. I would especially appreciate use of AK-class because I would enjoy the reference/continuity, but my critique would be addressed well enough by using pretty much anything except the 'generic' XK form.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad you like the crosslinks, Moose. There is actually one more link planned for when the contest ends and I can edit again, so that is a thing.

WRT to your nitpicks:

Nitpick #1: Unclear why Ferro is allowed to talk to Corbyn without a guard to prevent her from attacking him. Seems like a basic security measure.

Couple of reasons for this: 1) Corbyn is still holding up pretty well at this point, so there is no indicator that she's just going to outright attack Ferro. 2) Corbyn does have the fact that she is former SCP personnel providing her with a little more trust than the run of the mill D-class, so that is also a factor. But, you are right that just a standard bit of security would be to have at least one guard there, so I'll add that in once edits open back up again.

Nitpick #2: Using XK-Class End of the World Scenario to mean just any end of the world scenario is disappointingly mediocre worldbuilding, especially when it's something that clearly isn't a traditional end of the world scenario. But most likely this is something only a few people will care about.

I'm going to level with you, it wasn't until after the editing period ended that I found the more fitting AK-class End of World Scenario, which is arguably a much better fit than straight up XK-class. So I done goofed on my research there, and will likely switch it over once editing goes back online.

Thanks for the review.

EDIT: Now that the 3000 contest has closed, the above edits have been made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the crosslinks. The writing is good. I like the description of the various dream-humanoids, especially the ones in the logs (since the examples were mostly images I'd seen before).

But this is, overall, nothing I haven't seen before. And I agree with an earlier comment about payoff never coming. It's just what it is, which is honestly fairly generic; not much else there. I've seen this concept in lots of horror, and in other stuff on this site. Sure it's decently written, but not much else.

Given that it seems to be well-liked, there's no real pressure to be more ambitious with this, so take this feedback with as many grains of salt as you like.

Nitpick #1: Unclear why Ferro is allowed to talk to Corbyn without a guard to prevent her from attacking him. Seems like a basic security measure.

Nitpick #2: Using XK-Class End of the World Scenario to mean just any end of the world scenario is disappointingly mediocre worldbuilding, especially when it's something that clearly isn't a traditional end of the world scenario. But most likely this is something only a few people will care about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Daw!

Thanks, OZ.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really hope this gets some more attention. When I first read it as a draft it was one of my favorite entries, and throughout this whole contest, it still has been as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Between reading the draft and now, I've watched Madness Combat in its entirety a couple times and re-reading this made me think of Tricky for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. Two links is not much of a wikiwalk, but I'm glad you enjoyed being able to go down that rabbit hole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a great one to finish reading at stupid o clock in the morning when I can't sleep. I probably should have seen that coming with the title and the CPAP machine picture…

It also sent me spiraling across half the wiki chasing links, though, which was fun!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A few tweaks have been made to the article with respect to SCP-3000's effects on those with insomnia. Nothing major, but I feel adds another layer to the mystery of whats going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late response, but thanks OZ! Glad it still held up after a second (third?) read.

item.title: Re: Who needs to sleep anyway ?
item.content:encoded:
Be a strong, independent reader who don't need no sleep.

In all seriousness, glad you liked it.

item.title: Who needs to sleep anyway ?
item.content:encoded:
Like really ?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. Though I feel I should point out that this isn't Oneiroi, as much as its something pulling the wool over Oneiroi's eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also don't have any love for Oneiroi, though this at least doesn't hit my usual distaste for general dream skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And then the Oneiroi link came up and reminded me of that skip where dream-people take 'rides' in living peoples' bodies; the way the researcher describes her experiences sounds like almost the exact same thing, just not consenting.

I mean, kind of. The thing about this, is that several factors are leaked throughout the course of the article. Something or someone who can remotely kill people in their sleep has managed to gain access to the user data from the Oneiroi app, and using that labor constructed a device that allows these entities to parasitize the headspace of those infected. Now its loose, and the foundation often can't contain the infection outbreaks until after a fair number of people are broken as a result.

But, eh, if you didn't find it scary, you didn't find it scary. Thanks for the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I was waiting for a payoff that never came.

I do like the variety of humanoids this creates — I assume they're all manifestations of fears — and I, too, was amused by "a clown", but I couldn't shake the feeling it was all fairly standard stuff. And then the Oneiroi link came up and reminded me of that skip where dream-people take 'rides' in living peoples' bodies; the way the researcher describes her experiences sounds like almost the exact same thing, just not consenting. It's an article about fear, but it's not scary by itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some people, their fear personified is a figure roughly the size of a human infant composed entirely of fused teeth.

For others, it is a clown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The list of horrible, nightmarish humanoids being concluded by "a clown" made me laugh pretty hard. I'm not sure if that was intended, but all I can say is: well played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its good to be back in Skipland.

Glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome, scary stuff.

I always love your writing Conwell. Welcome back to Skipland.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What *really* put this over the edge for me was the large number of very detailed descriptions of the horrific things people saw.

People talk about vague amorphous scary stuff, but these were acute, and particular, and very detailed.

Aside from that, I have to echo a lot of what ARD said: This was tight, and well paced. Nothing felt wasted, and every section felt like it explored and explained what it was talking about, then finished before it felt overplayed. A good example being the initial bulleted list of entities. Enough to really get my skin crawling, but just enough that I felt like "I could have used one or two more examples, that would have been awesome" without feeling like it wasn't enough.

Really, really good stuff man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are very kind words, ARD.

Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good. The crosslinking is exceptional at introducing wrinkles into the story and fleshing out the world, the Group of Interest and associated MTF are used very well here, the interview is vivid and intense with powerful characters, and the pacing is tight and brisk. The other entries could learn a thing or two from this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh heeeeeellll yes, you posted it! Glad to see it's up, I loved reading this one over!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Well, here goes nothing. Its been almost a year since I've written a proper SCP article (tales are a hell of a drug, kids) and the 3000 Contest seemed like as good a time as any to try my hand at the bread and butter of the site again. Enjoy

Special thanks to all the following who reviewed this article when it was still a draft:

TyGently, Sirens, Cyantreuse, AbsentmindedNihilist, Zyn, Lazar Lyusternik, Lex1nat0r, minmin, OZ Ouroboros, LilyFlower, SpectralDragon, Agent MacLeod, ProcyonLotor, Shaggydredlocks, DrBleep, This Is War, DrMagnus, djkaktus, and tretter

Extra special bonus thanks to Roget, A Random Day, and Taffeta for all reviewing this and providing a ton of feedback. The article would not look half as polished as it currently does without you.

Image source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:CPAP-machine-resmedS9.jpg

Here is to a fun article to write, and a very fun rest of the contest.

JC
